title: Cain Murders Abel
description: Cain lured his brother Abel into the field and killed him, committing the first murder recorded in Scripture. God confronted Cain and cursed him to be a wandering fugitive upon the earth. Cain was marked by God and went to dwell in the land of Nod, east of Eden.

title: Lamech's Boast of the Sword
description: Lamech, a descendant of Cain and the first recorded polygamist, boasted to his wives Adah and Zillah that he had killed a man for wounding him. He declared that if Cain was avenged sevenfold, then Lamech would be avenged seventy-sevenfold. This song reflects the escalating violence of Cain's line.

title: Birth of Noah
description: Lamech (of Seth's line) became the father of a son whom he named Noah, saying he would bring comfort and rest from the toil and painful labor of the cursed ground. Noah's name means 'rest' or 'comfort,' foreshadowing his role in the new beginning after the Flood.

title: God Commands Noah to Build the Ark
description: God saw the wickedness of humanity and told Noah He would destroy all flesh with a flood. He commanded Noah to build an ark of gopher wood with precise dimensions and to bring his family and two of every animal aboard. Noah obeyed everything God commanded him.

title: The Rainbow Covenant with Noah
description: After the Flood, God established His covenant with Noah, his descendants, and every living creature, promising never again to destroy all life with a flood. God set the rainbow in the clouds as the sign of this everlasting covenant. This was the first formal covenant between God and all of creation.

title: Table of Nations: Noah's Sons
description: After the Flood, the sons of Noah — Shem, Ham, and Japheth — spread out across the earth, each becoming the ancestor of nations and peoples. The Table of Nations records 70 peoples who filled the earth, tracing the origins of the ancient world's nations from one family. This passage explains how the world was repopulated after the Flood.

title: Lot Separates from Abraham at Bethel
description: When strife arose between the herdsmen of Abraham and Lot because the land could not support both their flocks, Abraham generously offered Lot first choice of the land. Lot chose the well-watered Jordan plain toward Sodom, while Abraham remained in Canaan. This peaceful separation set the stage for Lot's later troubles in Sodom.

title: Abraham Rescues Lot: Battle of the Kings

location.name: Valley of Siddim, Dead Sea region

description: Four kings waged war against five kings of the cities of the plain, and Lot was taken captive along with his possessions. When Abraham heard that his nephew had been captured, he led 318 trained men and pursued the enemy as far as Dan, routing them and recovering Lot, all the goods, and all the people. This battle demonstrated Abraham's military prowess and family loyalty.

title: Melchizedek Blesses Abraham
description: After Abraham's victory over the kings, Melchizedek king of Salem — priest of God Most High — came out to meet him with bread and wine and blessed Abraham. Abraham gave Melchizedek a tenth of everything he had recovered. Melchizedek is a mysterious priestly-kingly figure whose priesthood prefigures Christ's eternal priesthood.

title: God's Covenant with Abraham (Stars and Smoking Pot)
description: God spoke to Abraham in a vision, promising him a son from his own body and descendants as numerous as the stars. Abraham believed God and it was credited to him as righteousness. God then formalized a covenant, passing through the divided animal carcasses as a smoking firepot and a flaming torch, binding Himself unilaterally to the promise of the land.

title: Hagar Flees to the Wilderness: Angel Appears
description: When Sarai dealt harshly with Hagar after Hagar conceived Abram's child, Hagar fled into the wilderness. The Angel of the Lord found her by a spring in the wilderness of Shur and told her to return and submit to Sarai, promising her son would be named Ishmael and be a great nation. Hagar called the Lord 'the God who sees me,' and the well was named Beer-lahai-roi.

title: Covenant of Circumcision
description: God appeared to Abram when he was 99 years old and changed his name to Abraham, meaning 'father of many nations,' and Sarai's name to Sarah. God instituted circumcision as the sign of the covenant between Himself and Abraham's descendants for all generations. God also confirmed that Sarah would bear a son named Isaac within a year.

title: Three Visitors Announce Isaac's Birth
description: Three men appeared to Abraham at Mamre and he offered them lavish hospitality. One of the visitors — revealed to be the Lord — announced that Sarah would have a son by this time next year. Sarah, listening from the tent door, laughed to herself in disbelief, but God asked, 'Is anything too hard for the Lord?'

title: Abraham Intercedes for Sodom
description: As the Lord's messengers departed toward Sodom, God revealed His plan to destroy it because of its great wickedness. Abraham boldly interceded, bargaining with God from fifty righteous people down to ten, asking if God would spare the city for the sake of the righteous within it. God agreed each time but not even ten righteous could be found in Sodom.

title: Lot Flees Sodom; Lot's Wife Becomes a Pillar of Salt
description: As dawn broke, the angels urgently pressed Lot to flee with his family before the city was destroyed. God rained down burning sulfur on Sodom and Gomorrah from the Lord out of the heavens, destroying everything in the cities and the surrounding plain. Lot's wife looked back and became a pillar of salt, a warning against disobedience and longing for a condemned way of life.

title: Lot's Daughters and the Birth of Moab and Ammon

location.name: Cave near Zoar, Dead Sea region

description: After fleeing Sodom, Lot and his daughters lived in a cave near Zoar. Fearing they were the last people alive, Lot's daughters devised a plan to preserve their family line by making Lot drunk and lying with him on successive nights. The elder daughter bore a son named Moab, ancestor of the Moabites, and the younger bore a son named Ben-ammi, ancestor of the Ammonites.

title: Abraham and Abimelech at Gerar
description: Abraham traveled to the Negev and settled between Kadesh and Shur, staying in Gerar. Again calling Sarah his sister, Abimelech king of Gerar took Sarah, but God warned Abimelech in a dream that Sarah was a man's wife. God healed Abimelech, his wife, and his servants after Abraham prayed, and Abraham received gifts and land in restitution.

title: Isaac Is Born at Beersheba
description: God kept His promise and Sarah conceived and bore Abraham a son in his old age, at the very time God had promised. Abraham named him Isaac and circumcised him when he was eight days old, just as God had commanded. Sarah declared that God had brought her laughter, and everyone who heard would laugh with her at this miraculous birth.

title: Ishmael Mocks Isaac; Hagar and Ishmael Sent Away
description: At Isaac's weaning feast, Sarah saw Ishmael mocking and demanded that Abraham send away Hagar and Ishmael so that Ishmael would not share the inheritance with Isaac. God told Abraham to listen to Sarah, promising to make Ishmael a nation because he was Abraham's son. Hagar and Ishmael were sent into the wilderness of Beersheba, where God again provided water and renewed His promise to Ishmael.

title: Abraham and Abimelech's Well Covenant at Beersheba
description: Abimelech came to Abraham with his commander Phicol and proposed a treaty between them, recognizing that God was with Abraham in all he did. Abraham agreed but complained about a well his servants had dug that Abimelech's men had seized. They made a covenant and Abraham set apart seven ewe lambs as a witness that he had dug the well; the place was called Beersheba, meaning 'well of the oath.'

title: God Commands Abraham to Sacrifice Isaac
description: God tested Abraham by commanding him to take his son Isaac — his only son, whom he loved — and offer him as a burnt offering on one of the mountains in the land of Moriah. Abraham rose early in the morning and set out with Isaac and two servants, trusting that God would provide. This test represented the ultimate trial of Abraham's faith.

title: Binding of Isaac on Mount Moriah
description: Abraham bound Isaac and placed him on the altar atop Mount Moriah, and raised the knife to slay his son. The Angel of the Lord called out from heaven and stopped him, declaring that Abraham had not withheld his only son and so God would bless him abundantly. God provided a ram caught in the thicket as the substitute sacrifice, and Abraham called the place 'The Lord Will Provide.'

title: Abraham's Servant Seeks a Wife for Isaac
description: Abraham charged his oldest servant with an oath not to take a wife for Isaac from the Canaanites but from among his relatives in his homeland. The servant loaded ten camels with gifts and set out for the city of Nahor in Mesopotamia. He prayed for a specific sign from God to identify the right woman, demonstrating faith in divine guidance.

title: Rebekah at the Well in Nahor
description: Abraham's servant prayed that the young woman who offered to water his camels would be the one chosen for Isaac. Before he had finished praying, Rebekah came out with her jar, gave him a drink, and offered to water all ten camels — fulfilling the sign exactly. Rebekah proved to be the granddaughter of Abraham's brother Nahor, confirming divine guidance.

title: Isaac and Rebekah Married
description: Isaac was walking in the fields at evening when he saw the camel caravan approaching. Rebekah veiled herself when she learned the man was Isaac, and the servant told Isaac all that had happened. Isaac brought Rebekah into his mother Sarah's tent, she became his wife, and he loved her; thus Isaac was comforted after his mother's death.

title: Jacob and Esau: Birthright Sold for Stew
description: Esau returned from the field famished and begged Jacob for some of his red stew. Jacob seized the moment and demanded Esau's birthright in exchange for the food. Esau, caring only for immediate physical need, swore away his birthright to Jacob and ate and drank, despising his birthright.

title: Isaac's Wells in Gerar and Beersheba
description: Isaac dug again the wells his father Abraham had dug, and herdsmen of Gerar quarreled over the first two wells, so he named them Esek and Sitnah. He moved on and dug another well and called it Rehoboth, saying God had made room for them. God appeared to Isaac at Beersheba that night, repeating the Abrahamic covenant, and Isaac built an altar there.

title: Isaac Blesses Jacob
description: Elderly Isaac, with failing eyesight, intended to bless his firstborn Esau but Rebekah devised a plan for Jacob to receive it instead. Jacob dressed in Esau's clothes and brought prepared food to deceive his father, and Isaac gave Jacob the blessing of abundance, nations serving him, and dominion over his brothers. When Esau returned and discovered the deception, Isaac trembled and Esau wept bitterly.

title: Jacob Flees to Haran
description: Esau hated Jacob for stealing his blessing and planned to kill him after their father's death. Rebekah warned Jacob and sent him to her brother Laban in Haran, telling Isaac she feared Jacob would marry a Hittite woman. Isaac blessed Jacob again and sent him to Paddan-aram to find a wife from among Laban's daughters.

title: Jacob's Dream of the Ladder at Bethel
description: Stopping for the night at Luz, Jacob used a stone as a pillow and dreamed of a stairway reaching to heaven with angels ascending and descending on it. The Lord stood above it, reaffirming to Jacob the Abrahamic covenant of land and descendants as numerous as dust. Jacob woke in awe, set up his stone as a pillar, poured oil on it, vowed to God, and named the place Bethel, meaning 'house of God.'

title: Jacob Meets Rachel at the Well
description: Jacob arrived in the land of the people of the East and found shepherds waiting at a well covered by a large stone. Rachel daughter of Laban arrived with her father's sheep, and Jacob single-handedly rolled the stone from the well's mouth and watered her flock. Jacob wept and kissed Rachel and told her he was her father's nephew; Rachel ran to tell Laban.

title: Jacob Marries Leah and Rachel
description: Jacob loved Rachel and agreed to serve Laban seven years for her, and the years seemed like only a few days because of his love for her. On the wedding night, Laban substituted Leah, and Jacob did not discover the deception until morning. Jacob then served another seven years and received Rachel as well, though he loved Rachel more than Leah.

title: Jacob's Twelve Sons Born in Haran
description: God saw that Leah was unloved and opened her womb while Rachel remained barren. Leah bore Reuben, Simeon, Levi, and Judah; then through the maidservants Bilhah and Zilpah came Dan, Naphtali, Gad, and Asher. Leah bore Issachar, Zebulun, and Dinah, and finally God remembered Rachel and she bore Joseph and later Benjamin, the twelve sons who would become the twelve tribes of Israel.

title: Jacob Prospers with Laban's Flocks
description: After Joseph's birth, Jacob negotiated with Laban to take the speckled and spotted animals as his wages, and Laban agreed. Through a divinely guided strategy using striped branches at the watering troughs, Jacob caused the stronger animals to bear striped, speckled, and spotted offspring. Jacob grew exceedingly prosperous, acquiring large flocks, servants, camels, and donkeys, while Laban's sons grew resentful.

title: Jacob Flees from Laban toward Canaan
description: God told Jacob to return to the land of his fathers, saying He had seen all that Laban had done to him. Jacob gathered his wives, children, and all his flocks and possessions and departed secretly while Laban was away shearing sheep. Rachel stole her father's household idols without Jacob's knowledge, and Jacob crossed the Euphrates heading toward the hill country of Gilead.

title: Laban Pursues Jacob; Covenant at Mizpah
description: Laban overtook Jacob in the hill country of Gilead, and after God warned Laban in a dream not to harm Jacob, they argued and then made a covenant of peace. They set up a heap of stones as a witness and called the place Mizpah, saying 'May the Lord watch between you and me when we are absent from one another.' They offered sacrifice, ate together, and parted in peace.

title: Jacob Prepares to Meet Esau at Mahanaim
description: Angels of God met Jacob at a place he called Mahanaim ('two camps'). Jacob sent messengers ahead to Esau and was terrified to learn his brother was coming with four hundred men. He divided his people and flocks into two camps for safety, prayed earnestly to God reminding Him of His promises, and sent lavish gifts ahead to appease Esau.

title: Jacob Wrestles with the Angel at the Jabbok
description: After sending his family across the Jabbok ford, Jacob was left alone and wrestled with a man — understood as a divine being — until daybreak. When the man could not overpower Jacob, he touched Jacob's hip socket and it was wrenched, yet Jacob refused to let go until the man blessed him. Jacob received the new name Israel, meaning 'he strives with God,' and he called the place Peniel, saying he had seen God face to face.

title: Jacob and Esau Reconcile
description: Jacob looked up and saw Esau approaching with his four hundred men, so he arranged his family in order and bowed to the ground seven times as he approached his brother. Esau ran to meet him and embraced him, fell on his neck, and they wept together, a stunning reconciliation after decades of estrangement. After parting peacefully, Jacob went to Succoth and built a house and shelters for his livestock.

title: Dinah and Shechem; Simeon and Levi's Revenge
description: Shechem son of Hamor the Hivite violated Dinah, Jacob's daughter, and then sought to marry her. While Jacob's sons appeared to negotiate peace, requiring circumcision of all men in the city, Simeon and Levi attacked the unsuspecting men on the third day when they were in pain and killed every male. Jacob was troubled, fearing reprisals, and the brothers defended their action as justice for their sister.

title: God Renews Covenant with Jacob at Bethel
description: God told Jacob to go to Bethel and build an altar there, and Jacob commanded his household to put away all foreign gods. God appeared to Jacob again at Bethel and blessed him, reconfirming his name as Israel, and renewed the covenant promises of fruitfulness, nations, and the land given to Abraham and Isaac. Jacob set up a stone pillar and poured oil and wine on it.

title: Rachel Dies Giving Birth to Benjamin
description: As Jacob's party journeyed from Bethel, Rachel went into difficult labor near Ephrath. The midwife assured her she would have another son, but Rachel died in childbirth. With her dying breath she named the child Ben-oni ('son of my sorrow'), but Jacob named him Benjamin ('son of the right hand'). Jacob set up a pillar over her grave, which remained there for generations.

title: Judah and Tamar
description: After Judah's sons Er and Onan died for their wickedness, he withheld his third son Shelah from marrying the widow Tamar. Tamar disguised herself as a cult prostitute and conceived by Judah himself. When Judah was told Tamar was pregnant, he called for her execution, but when she produced his seal and cord, Judah declared 'She is more righteous than I.' Tamar bore twins Perez and Zerah, and through Perez came the line of King David.

title: Joseph Falsely Accused by Potiphar's Wife
description: Joseph, who was handsome and well-built, caught the eye of Potiphar's wife, who repeatedly pressured him to sleep with her. Joseph refused, refusing to sin against God, and when she grabbed his cloak he fled, leaving it in her hand. She falsely accused Joseph of attempted rape using the cloak as evidence, and Potiphar had Joseph thrown into prison.

title: Joseph Interprets the Cupbearer and Baker's Dreams in Prison
description: Pharaoh's cupbearer and baker were imprisoned with Joseph, and each had a troubling dream. Joseph told them interpretations belong to God and interpreted their dreams: the cupbearer would be restored to his position in three days, while the baker would be hanged. Both came true exactly as Joseph said, but the restored cupbearer forgot Joseph and did not mention him to Pharaoh.

title: Joseph Interprets Pharaoh's Dreams
description: Two years after the cupbearer's restoration, Pharaoh dreamed of seven fat cows swallowed by seven lean cows, and seven plump heads of grain consumed by seven thin ones. The cupbearer finally remembered Joseph, who was brought from prison. Joseph explained that God was revealing seven years of abundance followed by seven years of severe famine, and advised Pharaoh to store grain during the good years.

title: Joseph Becomes Governor of Egypt
description: Pharaoh recognized that the Spirit of God was in Joseph and appointed him as second-in-command over all Egypt, giving him his signet ring, fine linen clothing, and a gold chain. Joseph was thirty years old when he entered Pharaoh's service and rode in a chariot as his second-in-command. During the seven years of abundance Joseph collected and stored massive amounts of grain, preparing Egypt for the coming famine.

title: Joseph's Brothers Come to Egypt the First Time
description: When the famine spread to Canaan, Jacob sent ten of his sons to Egypt to buy grain, keeping Benjamin home. Joseph recognized his brothers when they bowed before him but they did not recognize him, fulfilling his earlier dreams. Joseph tested them by accusing them of being spies, kept Simeon as a hostage, and required them to bring their youngest brother Benjamin on their return.

title: Joseph's Brothers Return to Egypt with Benjamin
description: When the grain from Egypt ran out, Jacob reluctantly allowed Benjamin to accompany his brothers back to Egypt, after Judah pledged his own life as surety for the boy's safety. Joseph saw Benjamin and was so moved he had to leave the room to weep privately. He had his steward hide his silver cup in Benjamin's sack, then accused the brothers of theft, testing whether they would abandon Benjamin as they had abandoned him.

title: Joseph Reveals Himself to His Brothers
description: When Judah offered himself in place of Benjamin, Joseph could no longer control himself and wept so loudly the Egyptians could hear. He cleared the room and revealed himself saying 'I am Joseph! Is my father still alive?' He urged his brothers not to be distressed over selling him, declaring that God had sent him ahead to save lives. He kissed all his brothers and wept over them, and they talked together.

title: Jacob and Family Move to Egypt
description: Israel set out with all his possessions and stopped at Beersheba to offer sacrifices to the God of his father Isaac. God spoke to him in a vision of the night, saying 'I am God, the God of your father; do not be afraid to go down to Egypt, for I will make you into a great nation there.' Jacob and all his descendants — seventy in all — went to Egypt, settling in the land of Goshen.

title: Jacob Blesses Joseph's Sons Ephraim and Manasseh
description: When Jacob was dying and learned Joseph was coming with his sons, he rallied his strength and sat up. Jacob adopted Ephraim and Manasseh as his own sons, equal to Reuben and Simeon in inheritance. Despite Joseph's protest, Jacob deliberately crossed his hands and placed his right hand on Ephraim the younger, giving him the greater blessing, saying the younger would be greater, and Ephraim's descendants would become a multitude of nations.

title: Jacob's Deathbed Blessings to His Twelve Sons
description: Jacob gathered all twelve sons and pronounced prophetic blessings and words over each, some corrective and some glorious. Judah received the most prominent blessing — the scepter would not depart from Judah until Shiloh comes, pointing to the Messianic king. Joseph was blessed with abundance above his brothers, and Benjamin called a ravenous wolf. These blessings shaped the identity of the twelve tribes for centuries.

title: Death of Jacob and Burial at Machpelah
description: Jacob died after charging his sons to bury him in the cave of Machpelah with Abraham, Sarah, Isaac, Rebekah, and Leah. Joseph wept over his father and had him embalmed over forty days. Pharaoh granted permission for a great mourning procession; Joseph, his brothers, Pharaoh's officials and elders traveled to Canaan where they buried Jacob in the cave of Machpelah as he had commanded.
